one_line_takeaway: A Google-authored survey proposing the 'Abuse Detection Lifecycle' taxonomy (Labeling, Detection, Review/Appeals, Auditing) to organize how LLMs are being used across the content-moderation pipeline, synthesizing production trade-offs and open challenges (cost/latency, over-refusal bias, unfaithful explanations, jailbreak vulnerability) from ~74 cited works rather than presenting new experiments.
key_finding: The survey's own synthesized conclusion (Section VII/VIII, not a single cited paper's finding) is that LLM integration exposes systemic 'production gaps' and biases across the pipeline: (1) a cost/latency 'production gap' between frontier LLM accuracy and the throughput platforms need; (2) an 'over-refusal paradox' where RLHF-aligned models (e.g., LLaMA-2-Chat, GPT-3.5) systematically over-predict abuse out of safety caution while instruction-tuned models (e.g., Flan-T5, OPT-IML) systematically under-predict it -- a bias direction the survey reports as consistent across datasets per the cited Zhang (2025) study; (3) a 'plausibility vs. faithfulness' gap where LLM-generated explanations can be fluent and convincing to human reviewers yet causally disconnected from the model's actual decision process (per cited Turpin 2023 on unfaithful CoT, and Wang 2023 on persuasion bias in explanation evaluation); and (4) that auditing itself is undermined by vendor model opacity/drift (black-box APIs that change without notice, per cited Pozzobon 2023).
core_contribution: This is a narrative literature survey (not an empirical study) by five Google engineers proposing the 'Abuse Detection Lifecycle' (ADL), a four-stage taxonomy for organizing how LLMs are used across platform Trust & Safety pipelines: (I) Label & Feature Generation (LLMs as 'data amplifiers' generating/augmenting training labels to solve the cold-start problem), (II) Detection (LLMs as 'specialized experts' for zero/few-shot classification, fine-tuned guard models, embedding-based/contrastive detection, and LLM+GNN network/narrative analysis), (III) Review & Appeals (LLMs as 'cognitive support assistants' generating explanations and summarizing evidence for human moderators), and (IV) Auditing & Governance (LLMs as 'auditors/meta-evaluators' doing adversarial stress-testing and bias audits). The paper's own contribution is the organizing framework itself, its synthesis of engineering trade-offs (latency, cost, determinism), and its per-stage 'challenges + future research directions' analysis; the specific technical results it discusses (numbers, systems, datasets) are all drawn from ~74 cited works, not produced by the authors.
threat_model: No single formal threat model is defined by the authors themselves (this is a survey, not an attack/defense paper). The paper does discuss, as a recurring theme drawn from cited literature, a 'dual-use/safety paradox': the same LLM capabilities that improve abuse detection also lower the barrier for attackers to generate scalable, personalized abuse, jailbreak prompts, disinformation, and coordinated inauthentic behavior. It surveys adversarial concerns reported elsewhere, including jailbreak/prompt-manipulation attacks against chatbot moderation (via the cited ToxicChat benchmark), instruction fine-tuning data-poisoning backdoors (via a cited influence-function detection paper), and chain-of-thought explanation unfaithfulness that could mislead human reviewers (via cited work on unfaithful CoT rationalization). None of these threat scenarios are original to this paper -- each is attributed to the specific cited source.
limitations: Limitations are those the authors themselves flag as open challenges for the field, not limitations of an empirical method (since none was run): reliance on autoregressive LLMs creates latency unsuitable for high-volume real-time moderation; general-purpose LLM judges exhibit over-refusal and prompt/artifact sensitivity (e.g., apologetic phrasing skewing safety judgments, per cited Chen & Goldfarb-Tarrant 2025); fine-tuning pipelines are vulnerable to data-poisoning backdoors from as little as 1% poisoned examples (per cited Li 2025); dense embeddings used for retrieval-augmented moderation risk leaking sensitive user attributes (per cited Mireshghallah & Li 2025); and auditing is hampered by model opacity, undisclosed vendor updates, and a narrow focus on accuracy metrics (F1) at the expense of social/political legitimacy. The paper itself does not run any evaluation to test or quantify these limitations -- it aggregates them from the cited literature. As a survey with no stated inclusion/exclusion methodology (no systematic search protocol, PRISMA-style criteria, or reproducibility artifact), the coverage is necessarily curated/selective rather than exhaustive, though the authors do not acknowledge this as a limitation explicitly.

datasets_used: None generated or used directly by this paper -- it is a survey. Datasets discussed as belonging to cited works include: ToxiGen (274,000 synthetic toxic/benign statements, Hartvigsen 2022), BASIL and BABE (human-labeled media-bias baselines referenced by Horych 2025's 48,330 LLM-synthetic-label dataset), Banking77 (financial text classification, Loukas 2024), ToxicChat (human-annotated jailbreak/toxicity examples for chatbot interactions, Lin 2023), HateCOT (52,000 CoT-annotated hate-speech examples, Nghiem & Daumé 2024), and Latent Hatred (implicit hate speech benchmark, ElSherief 2021).
quantitative_results: All quantitative figures below are results from cited papers, reported by this survey as synthesis, not results produced by this paper's own authors.

Horych (2025): classifiers trained on 48,330 LLM-generated synthetic media-bias labels performed close to or outperforming models trained on expert-labeled BASIL/BABE, and surpassed their own zero-shot teacher LLMs by 5-9% in Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC).
Loukas (2024): retrieval-augmented few-shot prompting retrieved only 2.2% of examples while matching/surpassing GPT-4 few-shot accuracy on Banking77, at roughly $700 cost for the Banking77 test set.

Yao (2024): personalized (trait-conditioned) LLM labeling produced 1.5-4.4% higher weighted F1 than baseline, but caused classification flips in up to 9% of cases when message content and user traits conflicted.

Irfan (2025): multimodal (audio+text) abuse-detection model reached 92% accuracy on a custom Red-Flag/Green-Flag conversation dataset, versus 86% (text-only BERT) and 88% (SBERT) baselines -- a roughly 4-6 point gain from adding vocal cues.
Cited GPT-4 zero-shot toxicity classification: F1 exceeding 0.75 on standard benchmarks, described as rivaling non-expert human inter-annotator agreement.
FraudSquad (cited LLM+GNN spam/coordination detector): reported 44% precision improvement over state-of-the-art baselines.
Li (2025, cited): as little as 1% poisoned fine-tuning examples can install exploitable backdoors in instruction-tuned models.
baselines_compared: No baselines are compared by this paper's own authors. Baselines discussed within cited studies include: BERT/RoBERTa/HateBERT fine-tuned classifiers versus LLM zero/few-shot judges; text-only BERT and SBERT versus multimodal audio+text models (Irfan 2025); expert-labeled BASIL/BABE versus LLM-synthetic-labeled training sets (Horych 2025); GPT-4 few-shot versus retrieval-augmented few-shot prompting (Loukas 2024); and Flan-T5/OPT-IML (instruction-tuned) versus LLaMA-2-Chat/GPT-3.5 (RLHF-aligned) for abuse-label prediction bias direction (Zhang 2025).
